comments.body: A complete and functioning minimal reproducible example, one that easily reproduced the problem, would be very nice to have, one that included imports, a functioning GUI, a main method, etc...
Are you targetting a specific os or should this be compatible to any os that runs java with graphical user interfaces?
@SpaceTrucker platform independent solutions are always preferred, but the target OS is Windows.
@HovercraftFullOfEels I updated the question with an MVCE. I don't think including the GUI piece would count as "minimal" as the problem is reproducible without it.
Is battery consumption important in your usecase? Because this could be an argument against using sleep with short durations.
@SpaceTrucker not particularly, but using sleep is generally a bad practice unless it's the only option.

I don't know anything about the Java API here, but in Win32, when any userland process calls CloseClipboard then Windows sends WM_DRAWCLIPBOARD to any hWnds registered with SetClipboardViewer (even though the Clipboard contents are not guaranteed to have changed) - it sounds like this is what FlavorListener is already doing though.

@Dai I wrote a test program which prints the clipboard owner whenever WM_DRAWCLIPBOARD or WM_CLIPBOARDUPDATE are sent. It seems each is only sent once per SetClipboardData call and in both circumstances the clipboard is owned by a window. There is no message sent when the clipboard is closed.

@CardinalSystem Hmm, that's odd. Can you share your test program (github gist, etc)? - I'm very confident about the veracity of what I wrote above because if you look at the disassembly of User32.dll, Win32u.dll and win32kfull.sys and follow CloseClipboard to ` NtUserCloseClipboard` to xxxCloseClipboard (sic), you'll se it calls xxxDrawClipboard which triggers the WM_DRAWCLIPBOARD. If i have the time I'll write a PoC.

@Dai here's the demo. If you can get a working C++ or C# proof-of-concept off the ground that demonstrates WM_DRAWCLIPBOARD being sent when CloseClipboard is called, that will be enough for me to work off of and I will give you the bounty. I just will need a makefile, .csproj, or .sln so I can compile the demo on my end.
Yes in windows OS atleast "learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/1695747/…("

answers.body:
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.datatransfer.*;
import java.util.Arrays;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;
import java.util.concurrent.ScheduledExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

public class ClipboardWatcher {

    private static final ScheduledExecutorService executor = Executors.newSingleThreadScheduledExecutor();

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Clipboard clipboard = Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getSystemClipboard();
        clipboard.addFlavorListener(e -> checkClipboardAsync(clipboard));

        System.out.println("Listening for clipboard changes... Press Ctrl+C to copy something.");
    }

    private static void checkClipboardAsync(Clipboard clipboard) {
        // Run this check off the Event Dispatch Thread
        executor.execute(() -> {
            int retries = 0;
            while (retries < 5) { // limit retries
                try {
                    boolean hasText = Arrays.stream(clipboard.getAvailableDataFlavors())
                                            .anyMatch(DataFlavor::isFlavorTextType);
                    System.out.println("Clipboard has text: " + hasText);
                    return; // success
                } catch (IllegalStateException ex) {
                    // Clipboard busy, wait and retry
                    retries++;
                    try {
                        TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS.sleep((long) Math.pow(2, retries) * 50); // exponential backoff
                    } catch (InterruptedException ignored) {}
                }
            }
            System.err.println("Clipboard is still busy after retries.");
        });
    }
}

Retry with Backoff (No Sleep on EDT)

  1. Windows offers SetClipboardViewer / AddClipboardFormatListener, but these only notify of changes, not when it’s released.

  2. Even native C++ developers can’t directly subscribe to “clipboard closed” events — they’d also poll or use retry logic.

  3. JNI could technically wrap OpenClipboard + retry loops in native code, but it’s not safer than the Java approach above.

    Optional: AWT-Safe Version (If You Have a GUI)

    If you want to safely update Swing components (e.g., toggle a button):

    SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> myButton.setEnabled(hasText));

    from within your success branch.

Recommended action: Use JDK 24 or later or ignore it. Don't try to check, if the cllipboard has been closed (as you suggested), it won't help.

Details:

I could reproduce your problem with JDK < 24, but not with JDK >= 24 (latest Versions released for Windows).

So I assume your issue relates to https://bugs.openjdk.org/browse/JDK-8353950

I would expect it to be fixed with 11.0.29, 17.0.17 and 21.0.9 (every LTS release from October 2025 or later), but I couldn't get hold of such a release for Windows yet.

From what I saw it is basically a race condition, of concurrent clipboard updates and reads. I recommend to use one of the latest JDKs and until then ignore it. You could additionally mitigate it (not totally eliminate it, but make it less likely), by adding a wait of 50-100ms before you read the clipboard after the FlavorListener has been triggered. From what I saw, you will get a correct state with the last FlavorEvent (with or without wait), but I give no 100% guarantee for that. For example, copying with the Chrome browser or Notepad++ worked fine, but OneNote for Windows 10 is pushing multiple changes to the clipboard, of which receiving the last one succeeds, but the receiving the intermediate events failed.

title: Can you detect when the clipboard has been closed?
body:
TL;DR: I want to detect when another application closes the system clipboard.

Scenario

I am using a FlavorListener to toggle a button when the clipboard content type changes. The listener is calling Clipboard#getAvailableDataFlavors, which will inconsistently throw an an exception when I copy data from other programs:

Exception in thread "AWT-EventQueue-0" java.lang.IllegalStateException: cannot open system clipboard
    at sun.awt.windows.WClipboard.openClipboard(Native Method)
    at sun.awt.datatransfer.SunClipboard.getClipboardFormatsOpenClose(SunClipboard.java:327)
    at sun.awt.datatransfer.SunClipboard.getAvailableDataFlavors(SunClipboard.java:168)

MVCE

To reproduce the problem, run the program below and copy data to your system clipboard from another program (you may need multiple copy attempts before the exception is thrown):

import java.awt.Toolkit;
import java.awt.datatransfer.Clipboard;
import java.awt.datatransfer.DataFlavor;
import java.awt.datatransfer.FlavorListener;
import java.util.Arrays;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Foo { 

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Clipboard clipboard = Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getSystemClipboard();
        FlavorListener listener = e -> System.out.print("\rClipboard has text: "
                + Arrays.stream(clipboard.getAvailableDataFlavors()).anyMatch(DataFlavor::isFlavorTextType)
                + "\nPress enter to exit...");
        clipboard.addFlavorListener(listener);

        System.out.print("Press enter to exit...");
        new Scanner(System.in).nextLine();
    }

}

Findings

Internally, AWT's Clipboard is using the OpenClipboard function from winuser.h. According to the documentation, "OpenClipboard fails if another window has the clipboard open." Looking at the underlying C++ code for AWT clipboard, we can see that this is exactly the cause of the IllegalStateException.

I found these similar questions when researching this:

  • In Java why do i get java.lang.IllegalStateException: cannot open system clipboard
  • listen to clipboard changes, check ownership?

In both scenarios, the solution (which came from a Coderanch thread) was to use Thread#sleep(long) to allow time for the other application to close the clipboard.

Objections

I prefer not rely on sleep because there is no way of knowing with certainty when (or if!) the clipboard will be closed. Additionally:

  1. If you don't sleep for long enough, the IllegalStateException will still occur.
  2. A sleep duration which works for one machine is not guaranteed to work on a different machine.
  3. A sleep duration which successfully waits for one application to close the clipboard is not guaranteed to work for another application.
  4. If you "play it safe" and use a very long sleep duration:
    • The user may have to wait for the button to be enabled.
    • The user may click the button before it is disabled (causing an error to occur).
  5. Sleeping on the AWT Event thread will freeze the UI, preventing the user from performing other actions which are not dependent on the clipboard.
    • I would have to utilize more system resources to create a worker thread just to perform the sleep.

Question

I would like to know if there is a listener or callback that can be registered to execute when the clipboard has been closed. Does anything like this exist?

To clarify, I am open to the possibility of writing my own shared/dynamic link library if I need to utilize a native function to achieve this.
